text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 14 December 2020 

 Dr Amelia Shah  
 Riverside Medical Practice 

 12 Wharf Street, London, SE8 3AB 
Dear  Dr Amelia Shah 
 Alex Carter , DOB: 04-03-1999, Hospital No:  H729384  NHS No.  9075632198 
 Flat 3, 25 Kingfisher Court, London, SE14 5DN  

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He is a university student approaching examinations and reports prolonged study periods with variable sleep. The history is primarily patient-reported, corroborated in part by a friend via telephone during today’s consultation.

He describes two stereotyped events from sleep this year, without clear preceding illness. The first seizure was reported in June 2020 while visiting relatives in Canada. It happened during an afternoon nap, with an unusual tingling spreading from the right hand to the shoulder. The second event took place in December 2020 in the USA, again from sleep, lasting approximately five minutes, with comparable symptoms. He reports tongue biting on the second occasion and post-event confusion for around 20–30 minutes. There is no consistent daytime trigger recalled, though he notes marked sleep deprivation in the days preceding each event.

He currently takes no regular antiepileptic medication. There is no known family history of epilepsy. He denies alcohol or recreational drug use; caffeine intake is high during revision periods. Neurological examination today was normal. Interim GP-arranged blood tests (FBC, U&E, LFTs, glucose) last week were within normal limits by report. He has not yet had EEG or neuroimaging.

On the balance of the clinical description and sleep-related onset with generalised features (tongue bite, prolonged post-ictal phase) in a young adult, I suspect he has generalised epilepsy. Given the limited number of events and the clear association with sleep loss, we discussed risk reduction while urgent investigations are arranged. I have requested an MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) and an EEG including a sleep-deprived study. Seizure first aid and SUDEP risk were discussed in general terms.

For now, I have not initiated treatment pending EEG/MRI results and further characterisation; the patient prefers to avoid medication during exams if safe to do so, and understands the importance of sleep regularity. I advised strict sleep hygiene, avoidance of alcohol, and to limit caffeine. He should not drive and is aware of DVLA requirements to notify and refrain from driving following a first unprovoked seizure; we discussed this explicitly. University adjustments (exam arrangements and rest periods) were recommended and a supporting note will be provided.

Safety planning was reviewed: friends and flatmates have been informed; he will obtain a medical ID and download a seizure diary app to document any further events, including duration, recovery, and any injuries. He has been given emergency guidance for prolonged events.

I will review him in clinic after the EEG and MRI, or sooner if he has further seizures, daytime events, or any injury. If investigations support generalised epilepsy and events recur, we will discuss starting a first-line broad-spectrum antiseizure medication.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He describes two stereotyped events from sleep this year

The first seizure was reported in June 2020 while visiting relatives in Canada. It happened during an afternoon nap, with an unusual tingling spreading from the right hand to the shoulder. The second event took place in December 2020 in the USA, again from sleep, lasting approximately five minutes, with comparable symptoms

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter clearly states the patient had two seizures in 2020: one in June 2020 and another in December 2020. Clinic date is 14 December 2020, so the observation window from first to most recent event is approximately 6 months (June to December). He is not seizure-free for 6 months because the last event occurred in December 2020. Therefore, the inferred frequency is 2 seizures per 6 months.
seizure_frequency_number: He describes two stereotyped events from sleep this year
